facts.text: Elizabeth Keckley was born a slave.
Elizabeth Keckley was the child of slave parents.
Elizabeth Keckley's birthplace was Dinwiddie Court-House, Virginia.
Elizabeth Keckley's master was Col. A. Burwell.
Elizabeth Keckley was approximately four years old when she first began to remember.
Elizabeth Keckley's first duty was to take care of Col. Burwell's daughter, also named Elizabeth.
Elizabeth Keckley was raised to rely upon herself and to prepare herself to render assistance to others.
Elizabeth Keckley was transferred from a rude cabin to the household of her master due to her duty caring for the baby.
Elizabeth Keckley wore a short dress and a little white apron as her simple attire.
Elizabeth Keckley was lashed for the first time when she accidentally dropped the baby she was caring for.
Elizabeth Keckley grew strong and healthy despite her enslaved status.
Elizabeth Keckley knitted socks and attended to various kinds of work.
Elizabeth Keckley was told at age fourteen that she would never be worth her salt.
Elizabeth Keckley's mother was named Agnes.
Elizabeth Keckley's mother was kind and forbearing.
Elizabeth Keckley's mother was her only parent living with her for most of her childhood.
Elizabeth Keckley's father was the slave of another man.
Elizabeth Keckley's father was only allowed to visit her mother twice a year, during Easter holidays and Christmas.
Elizabeth Keckley's father was separated from her mother and sister when Mr. Burwell moved from Dinwiddie.
Elizabeth Keckley's father called her 'Little Lizzie.'
Elizabeth Keckley's parents were permanently separated when her father was ordered to go West with his master.
Elizabeth Keckley's father and mother never met again in this world.
Elizabeth Keckley's parents kept up a regular correspondence for years after their separation.
Mrs. Burwell was a hard task-master.
Elizabeth Keckley was intimately associated with Mrs. Abraham Lincoln during the most eventful periods of her life.
Elizabeth Keckley was Mrs. Lincoln's confidante.
Elizabeth Keckley served as a modiste (dressmaker).
Elizabeth Keckley spent thirty years as a slave.
Elizabeth Keckley spent four years in the White House.
Elizabeth Keckley was on the shady side of forty when writing her autobiography.
Elizabeth Keckley has vivid and distinct recollections of her childhood.
Elizabeth Keckley believes her life has been full of romance and eventful incidents.
Elizabeth Keckley states that everything she has written in her autobiography is strictly true.
Elizabeth Keckley has kind, true-hearted friends in both the South and the North.
Elizabeth Keckley would not wound her Southern friends by sweeping condemnation despite having been a slave.
Elizabeth Keckley was robbed of her liberty by a cruel custom.
Elizabeth Keckley rebelled against the robbery of her dearest right.
Elizabeth Keckley believes God rules the Universe.
Elizabeth Keckley views herself as a feeble instrument in God's hands.
Elizabeth Keckley believes she aided in bringing a solemn truth to the surface regarding slavery.
Elizabeth Keckley can afford to be charitable regarding her past suffering.
Elizabeth Keckley was prompted by the purest motive in writing about Mrs. Lincoln.
Elizabeth Keckley believes Mrs. Lincoln forced herself into notoriety by her own acts.
Elizabeth Keckley believes Mrs. Lincoln stepped beyond the formal lines which hedge about a private life.
Elizabeth Keckley believes Mrs. Lincoln was traduced in the public prints of the country.
Elizabeth Keckley believes Mrs. Lincoln may have been imprudent but had good intentions.
Elizabeth Keckley has been intimately associated with Mrs. Lincoln in the most eventful periods of her life.
Elizabeth Keckley has been a party to all of Mrs. Lincoln's movements.
Elizabeth Keckley excluded everything of a personal character from Mrs. Lincoln's letters in her publication.
Elizabeth Keckley published Mrs. Lincoln's letters to defend both Mrs. Lincoln and herself.
Elizabeth Keckley is not the special champion of the widow of President Lincoln.
Elizabeth Keckley has written with utmost frankness regarding Mrs. Lincoln.
Elizabeth Keckley has exposed Mrs. Lincoln's faults as well as given her credit for honest motives.
Elizabeth Keckley lived at 14 Carroll Place, New York as of March 14, 1868.
Elizabeth Keckley's father was sold away from her family.
Elizabeth Keckley's mother was deeply distressed by her husband's sale.
Elizabeth Keckley's mother and father never met again after his sale.
Elizabeth Keckley's parents maintained a regular correspondence for years after their separation.
Elizabeth Keckley's father wrote letters expressing love and hope of future reunion.
Elizabeth Keckley's father lived in Shelbyville, Tennessee.
Elizabeth Keckley's father died without ever seeing his wife and child again.
Elizabeth Keckley's father worked to earn money for his own benefit while enslaved.
Elizabeth Keckley's father hoped to purchase his own time from his master.
Elizabeth Keckley's father was a Christian who prayed regularly.
Elizabeth Keckley witnessed the sale of a human being when she was about seven years old.
Elizabeth Keckley lived at Prince Edward, Virginia during her childhood.
A child named Little Joe was sold by weight like hogs at Elizabeth Keckley's master's house.
Little Joe's mother was deceived about her son's sale.
Little Joe's mother was whipped for grieving over her lost child.
Colonel Burwell owned about seventy slaves at one time.
Colonel Burwell sold the majority of his slaves, separating families.
Colonel Burwell punished slaves for displaying sorrowful expressions.
Elizabeth Keckley's uncle lost a pair of ploughlines and hanged himself rather than face punishment.
Elizabeth Keckley's uncle was a slave of Colonel Burwell.
Elizabeth Keckley went to live with her master's eldest son when she was about fourteen years old.
Elizabeth Keckley's master's eldest son was a Presbyterian minister.
The Presbyterian minister had a small salary.
The Presbyterian minister's wife was morbidly sensitive about her poor parentage.
Elizabeth Keckley was loaned to the Presbyterian minister's household rather than sold.
Elizabeth Keckley did the work of three servants in the minister's household.
Elizabeth Keckley was nearly eighteen when the household moved to Hillsboro', North Carolina.
The Presbyterian minister took charge of a church in Hillsboro', North Carolina.
Mr. Bingham was a village schoolmaster and member of the Presbyterian minister's church.
Mr. Bingham was a hard and cruel man.
The Presbyterian minister's wife sought vengeance on Elizabeth Keckley.
Mr. Bingham flogged Elizabeth Keckley without just cause.
Elizabeth Keckley resisted Mr. Bingham's attempt to flog her.
Elizabeth Keckley was eighteen years old and fully developed when Mr. Bingham attempted to flog her.
Elizabeth Keckley did not scream during her flogging by Mr. Bingham.
Elizabeth Keckley was proud and refused to show her suffering to her tormentor.
The Presbyterian minister struck Elizabeth Keckley with a chair after she was flogged by Mr. Bingham.
Elizabeth Keckley could not sleep the night after being flogged and struck.
Elizabeth Keckley felt mental and bodily torture after the beatings.
Mr. Bingham pledged himself to subdue Elizabeth Keckley's 'stubborn pride'.
Mr. Bingham attempted to flog Elizabeth Keckley a second time on Friday.
Elizabeth Keckley bit Mr. Bingham's finger severely during their struggle.
Mr. Bingham beat Elizabeth Keckley with a heavy stick.
Mr. Bingham attempted to flog Elizabeth Keckley a third time on Thursday.
Mr. Bingham wept and asked Elizabeth Keckley's forgiveness after their final struggle.
Mr. Bingham never struck another servant after his final encounter with Elizabeth Keckley.
The Presbyterian minister attempted to beat Elizabeth Keckley with an oak broom handle.
The Presbyterian minister's wife begged him to stop beating Elizabeth Keckley.
Elizabeth Keckley was unable to leave her bed for five days after being beaten by the Presbyterian minister.
The Presbyterian minister promised never to strike Elizabeth Keckley again and kept his word.
The beatings of Elizabeth Keckley created a sensation in the town and neighborhood.
Elizabeth Keckley was regarded as fair-looking for one of her race.
A white man persecuted Elizabeth Keckley for four years with base designs.
Elizabeth Keckley became a mother as a result of persecution by a white man.
Elizabeth Keckley had only one child in her life.
Elizabeth Keckley did not wish to give birth to her child.
Elizabeth Keckley wrote a letter to her mother dated April 10, 1838 from Hillsboro'.
Elizabeth Keckley's mother had moved to Marsfield.
Elizabeth Keckley's family had previously lived at Boyton.
Elizabeth Keckley thought highly of the 'brick-house people'.
Elizabeth Keckley felt forgotten by her family.
Elizabeth Keckley did not expect to see her mother again.
Miss Anna was planning to go to Petersburg the following winter.
Miss Anna did not intend to take Elizabeth Keckley to Petersburg.
Elizabeth Keckley wished she lived where she could never see her mother to avoid disappointed hopes.
Elizabeth Keckley was formerly named Elizabeth Hobbs.
She lived in North Carolina before moving to Virginia.
She lived with Mr. Garland, who had married Miss Anne Burwell, one of her old master's daughters.
Mr. Garland moved to St. Louis hoping to improve his fortune in the West.
Elizabeth's mother had been raised in the family and cared for the children from infancy to maturity.
Elizabeth refused to allow her mother to be placed in service with strangers.
Elizabeth worked as a seamstress and dress-maker in St. Louis.
The best ladies in St. Louis were her patrons.
Elizabeth supported seventeen persons for two years and five months with her needle work.
She met Mr. Keckley in Virginia and came to regard him with more than friendship.
Mr. Keckley proposed marriage to her.
Elizabeth initially refused Mr. Keckley's proposal because she could not bear the thought of bringing children into slavery.
Elizabeth had a son.
Elizabeth's son had both Anglo-Saxon and African blood.
Elizabeth proposed to buy herself and her son, but the proposition was declined.
Mr. Garland offered her a quarter dollar to cross the river to freedom, which she refused.
Mr. Garland later agreed to sell her freedom and her son's for $1200.
Elizabeth consented to marry Mr. Keckley after learning she could purchase her freedom.
The wedding ceremony took place in the parlor with Mr. Garland giving her away.
Bishop Hawks performed the wedding ceremony.
Mr. Keckley proved to be dissipated and a burden rather than a help-mate.
Elizabeth later discovered that Mr. Keckley was a slave, not a free man as he had represented.
Elizabeth lived with Mr. Keckley for eight years.
Mr. Garland died while Elizabeth was still working to purchase her freedom.
Mr. Burwell, a Mississippi planter, came to St. Louis to settle Mr. Garland's estate.
Mr. Burwell was kind-hearted and said Elizabeth should be free.
Elizabeth planned to go to New York to appeal to the benevolence of people for money to buy her freedom.
Mrs. Garland required the names of six gentlemen who would vouch for Elizabeth's return.
Elizabeth obtained five signatures from gentlemen in St. Louis.
Mr. Farrow refused to sign, predicting that Elizabeth would not return from the North.
Mrs. Le Bourgois, one of Elizabeth's patrons, offered to raise the twelve hundred dollars among her friends instead of allowing Elizabeth to beg in New York.
Mrs. Le Bourgois contributed two hundred dollars of her own money toward Elizabeth's freedom.
The twelve hundred dollars were successfully raised by Mrs. Le Bourgois and others.
Elizabeth and her son George were freed on June 27, 1855.
Elizabeth was known in St. Louis as Lizzie Garland and later as Lizzie Keckley.
Her son George was known in St. Louis as Garland's George.
Elizabeth valued the respect and trust of those she esteemed.
Elizabeth believed in following the laws of the land regarding her enslavement and freedom.
Elizabeth Keckley accepted $1,200 as a loan to purchase freedom for herself and her son.
Elizabeth Keckley paid back the entire $1,200 loan in a short time through her work.
Elizabeth Keckley's husband was a source of trouble and burden to her.
Elizabeth Keckley worked too closely with her needle, which had negative effects on her health.
Elizabeth Keckley determined to separate from her husband due to his persistent dissipation.
Elizabeth Keckley left St. Louis in the spring of 1860.
Elizabeth Keckley traveled to Baltimore after leaving St. Louis.
Elizabeth Keckley attempted to form classes for young colored women to teach dress cutting and fitting in Baltimore.
Elizabeth Keckley's Baltimore scheme was not successful.
Elizabeth Keckley traveled to Washington, D.C. after Baltimore.
Elizabeth Keckley obtained work in Washington at two dollars and a half per day.
Elizabeth Keckley was initially required to obtain a license to remain in Washington for more than ten days.
Elizabeth Keckley called on Miss Ringold for assistance with her licensing situation.
Miss Ringold helped Elizabeth Keckley arrange to remain in Washington without paying for a license.
Elizabeth Keckley rented apartments in a good locality in Washington.
Mrs. Jefferson Davis came to Washington from the South in November of 1860.
Elizabeth Keckley was employed by Mrs. Davis on the recommendation of Mrs. Captain Hetsill.
Elizabeth Keckley arranged to work for Mrs. Davis half the day and for other patrons the other half.
Elizabeth Keckley came to Mrs. Davis's house every day after 12 noon.
Secret political meetings were held at Senator Davis's house almost every night.
Elizabeth Keckley worked on Christmas Eve to finish a dressing gown for Mr. Davis.
Elizabeth Keckley finished the dressing gown at a quarter to twelve on Christmas Eve.
Senator Davis appeared careworn and nervous when he came in on Christmas Eve.
Senator Davis inferred the gown was his Christmas present from his wife.
Elizabeth Keckley believed the dressing gown was worn by Mr. Davis during his presidency of the Confederate States.
Mrs. Davis expressed reluctance about leaving Washington and going South.
Mrs. Davis said she would rather be kicked about in Washington than be Mrs. President in the South.
Mrs. Davis offered to take Elizabeth Keckley South with her.
Mrs. Davis predicted war between the North and South.
Mrs. Davis believed the South would win the war.
Mrs. Davis predicted the Southern people would march on Washington and she would live in the White House.
Elizabeth Keckley considered Mrs. Davis's offer to go South but ultimately declined.
Elizabeth Keckley believed the North was strong and would fight for the flag.
Elizabeth Keckley decided to cast her lot with the North rather than go South.
Elizabeth Keckley made two chintz wrappers for Mrs. Davis before she left Washington.
Mrs. Davis said she must give up expensive dressing due to the impending war.
Elizabeth Keckley finished fine needle-work for Mrs. Davis and forwarded it to Montgomery, Alabama in June.
Elizabeth Keckley never met any of the Davis family after they left Washington in early 1860.
Elizabeth Keckley was in Chicago in the winter of 1865.
Elizabeth Keckley visited a charity fair in Chicago held for families of soldiers killed or wounded in the war.
Elizabeth Keckley recognized one of her chintz wrappers on a wax figure of Jefferson Davis at the Chicago fair.
Elizabeth Keckley's recognition of the dress caused great cheering and excitement at the fair.
Elizabeth Keckley left the building to escape the embarrassing situation at the fair.
Elizabeth Keckley inferred the dress was taken from Mrs. Davis's trunks rather than worn by Mr. Davis when captured.
Elizabeth Keckley made a chintz wrapper for Mrs. Davis before Davis departed from Washington for the South.
Keckley recognized a dress on a wax figure at a fair in Chicago as one she had made for Mrs. Davis.
Keckley's recognition of the dress caused great excitement and made her the object of deep curiosity.
Keckley left the building to escape an embarrassing situation caused by the crowd's attention.
Keckley inferred that the dress on the wax figure was taken from Mrs. Davis's trunks rather than worn by Mr. Davis.
Since arriving in Washington, Keckley had a great desire to work for the ladies of the White House.
Keckley was willing to make almost any sacrifice consistent with propriety to work for White House ladies.
Work came in slowly for Keckley when she first arrived in Washington.
Keckley felt embarrassed about meeting bills she could not pay.
Keckley met Mrs. Captain Lee at the Ringolds' house.
Miss Ringold recommended Keckley to make a dress for Mrs. Lee for a dinner party honoring the Prince of Wales.
Mrs. Lee's husband gave Keckley one hundred dollars in bank bills to purchase trimmings.
Keckley purchased laces from Harper & Mitchell.
Mr. Harper allowed Keckley to carry laces to Mrs. Lee for approval.
Mr. Harper told Keckley he believed her face was the index to an honest heart.
Keckley received a commission of twenty-five dollars from Mr. Harper on the purchase.
Mrs. Lee attracted great attention at the dinner party in her elegant dress.
Keckley received numerous orders after Mrs. Lee wore her dress, relieving her from pecuniary embarrassments.
Mrs. General McClean was a daughter of General Sumner.
Mrs. McClean drove up to Keckley's apartments and demanded a dress be made by Sunday.
Mrs. McClean offered to help Keckley obtain the privilege of working for Mrs. Lincoln in exchange for completing her dress by Sunday.
Keckley employed assistants to help complete Mrs. McClean's dress.
Mrs. Lincoln had upset a cup of coffee on her dress designed for the reception after Lincoln's inauguration.
Mrs. Lincoln had heard of Keckley's work from lady friends in St. Louis.
Keckley was summoned to meet Mrs. Lincoln at Willard's Hotel on Inauguration day.
Keckley crossed the threshold of the White House for the first time on Tuesday morning at eight o'clock.
Three mantua-makers were waiting to interview Mrs. Lincoln when Keckley arrived at the White House.
Keckley had worked for Mrs. Senator Davis in the city.
Mrs. Lincoln said she and her family were just from the West and were poor.
Mrs. Lincoln could not afford to pay big prices for dressmaking.
Keckley measured Mrs. Lincoln and took a bright rose-colored moire-antique dress to fit.
Mrs. Lincoln's relatives present included her sisters Mrs. Edwards and Mrs. Kellogg, and her nieces Elizabeth Edwards and Julia Baker.
The levee was postponed from Friday night to Tuesday night.
Mrs. Lincoln initially refused to go down to the levee, saying she had nothing to wear.
Keckley offered to dress Mrs. Lincoln, and she consented after being urged by Mrs. Grimsly and Mrs. Edwards.
Mr. Lincoln quoted poetry while preparing for the levee.
Tad displaced Mrs. Lincoln's lace handkerchief just before the levee.
Mrs. Lincoln wore a pearl necklace, pearl ear-rings, pearl bracelets, and red roses in her hair to the levee.
Keckley was surprised at Mrs. Lincoln's grace and composure at the levee.
Keckley had heard malicious reports of Mrs. Lincoln's low life, ignorance, and vulgarity before meeting her.
Keckley found that Mrs. Lincoln comported herself with calmness and dignity at the levee.
Keckley became the regular modiste of Mrs. Lincoln.
Keckley made fifteen or sixteen dresses for Mrs. Lincoln during the spring and early summer.
Mrs. Lincoln left Washington to spend hot weather at Saratoga, Long Branch, and other places.
Keckley was employed by Mrs. Senator Douglas, Mrs. Secretary Wells, Mrs. Secretary Stanton, and others.
Mrs. Douglas always dressed in deep mourning with excellent taste.
Several leading ladies of Washington society were extremely jealous of Mrs. Douglas's superior attractions.
Mrs. Lincoln returned to Washington in November.
The war was in progress when Mrs. Lincoln returned to Washington.
Some people had an intense desire to penetrate the inner circle of the White House.
Mr. Lincoln had grown up in the wilds of the West.
Evil report had said much of Mr. Lincoln and his wife.
The polite world was shocked by Mr. Lincoln and his family.
As soon as it was known that Keckley was Mrs. Lincoln's modiste, parties crowded around and affected friendship with her.
People hoped to induce Keckley to betray the secrets of the White House domestic circle.
A woman drove up to Keckley's rooms, gave her an order to make a dress, and insisted on partly paying in advance.
The woman called on Keckley every day and was exceedingly kind.
The woman expressed a desire to become an inmate of the White House.
The woman said she had heard of Mr. Lincoln's goodness and wanted to be near him.
The woman was willing to go to the White House as a menial if necessary.
The woman asked Keckley to recommend her to Mrs. Lincoln as a friend out of employment for a chambermaid position.
The woman offered to reward Keckley well if she would make the recommendation.
Elizabeth Keckley was Mrs. Lincoln's modiste.
Elizabeth Keckley was with Mrs. Lincoln every day or two.
A woman offered Elizabeth Keckley several thousand dollars to recommend her to Mrs. Lincoln as a chambermaid.
Elizabeth Keckley refused the bribe and told the woman she would sooner throw herself into the Potomac River than betray her employer's trust.
The woman who offered the bribe was an actress.
The actress's object was to enter the White House as a servant, learn its secrets, and publish a scandal.
Elizabeth Keckley indignantly refused every bribe offered to her.
Mrs. Lincoln's first public appearance that winter was at a New Year's Day reception.
Mrs. Lincoln called Elizabeth Keckley 'Lizabeth' after dropping the E from her name.
Mrs. Lincoln proposed giving three large receptions instead of state dinners to save money during wartime.
Mr. Lincoln initially opposed Mrs. Lincoln's plan but was persuaded by her arguments.
The reception was scheduled for February.
Willie Lincoln received a pony as a gift and insisted on riding it every day.
Willie Lincoln developed a severe cold from exposure to changeable weather, which deepened into fever.
Willie Lincoln was of a delicate constitution.
Willie Lincoln was Mrs. Lincoln's favorite child.
Willie Lincoln had a literary taste and was a studious boy.
Willie Lincoln wrote a poem about Colonel Edward Baker's death.
Dr. Stone pronounced Willie better and said there was every reason for early recovery.
Dr. Stone advised that the reception should proceed as planned.
Mrs. Lincoln wore a white satin dress trimmed with black lace to the reception.
Mr. Lincoln made a comment about Mrs. Lincoln's dress having a long tail.
Mr. Lincoln vetoed a suggestion that young people dance at the reception.
Mrs. Lincoln came upstairs several times during the reception to check on Willie.
Willie Lincoln died a few days after the reception.
Mr. Lincoln was deeply grief-stricken when he saw Willie's body.
Mr. Lincoln said Willie was too good for this earth and that God had called him home.
Mrs. Lincoln's grief was inconsolable and she did not attend Willie's funeral.
Willie was laid to rest in the cemetery.
The White House was draped in mourning after Willie's death.
Elizabeth Keckley had lost her own son before Willie's death.
Elizabeth Keckley's son went to the battlefield with three months troops and was killed in Missouri.
Elizabeth Keckley's son was found dead on the battlefield where General Lyon fell.
Mrs. Lincoln wrote a kind and comforting letter to Elizabeth Keckley about her son's death.
Nathaniel Parker Willis wrote a sketch of Willie Lincoln after his death.
Willie Lincoln was ten years old at the time described by Willis.
Willie Lincoln had extraordinary self-possession for a boy his age.
Willie Lincoln resembled his father in his genial and open expression.
Willie Lincoln was loved like an idol by the servants and nurses.
Willie Lincoln was his father's favorite child.
Mr. Lincoln and Willie were often seen hand in hand.
Willie was embalmed to be sent home to the West.
Mrs. Lincoln pasted Willis's sketch in a scrap-book with many tears.
In the summer of 1862, freedmen began to flock into Washington from Maryland and Virginia.
Freedmen came to Washington with hope in their hearts and their worldly goods on their backs.
Freedmen came to the Capital looking for liberty.
The North is not warm and impulsive in its reception of freedmen.
For every kind word spoken to freedmen, two harsh ones were uttered.
The bright joyous dreams of freedom faded for enslaved people in the presence of reality.
Many freedmen came to the Capital looking for liberty but did not know it when they found it.
The transition from slavery to freedom was too sudden for the freedmen.
Elizabeth Keckley was walking the streets of Washington when she heard music from Mrs. Farnham's house.
Mrs. Farnham was giving a festival for the benefit of sick and wounded soldiers.
Elizabeth Keckley suggested that a society of colored people be formed to labor for the benefit of unfortunate freedmen.
The idea of forming a relief society proved popular among colored people.
The Contraband Relief Association was organized with forty working members within two weeks.
In September of 1862, Mrs. Lincoln left Washington for New York.
Mrs. Lincoln requested Elizabeth Keckley to follow her to the Metropolitan Hotel in New York.
Mrs. Lincoln gave a subscription of $200 to the relief society.
Elizabeth Keckley was called to Boston by Mrs. Lincoln to visit her son Robert.
Robert Lincoln was attending college in Boston.
Elizabeth Keckley met Wendell Phillips and other Boston philanthropists.
A mass meeting was held at the Colored Baptist Church in Boston.
Rev. Mr. Grimes raised money and organized a branch society in Boston.
Mrs. Grimes and Mrs. Martin organized the Boston branch of the relief society.
The Boston branch sent over eighty large boxes of goods during the war.
A successful meeting was held at the Shiloh Church in New York.
Rev. Henry Highland Garnet was the pastor of Shiloh Church.
Robert Thompson, Steward of the Metropolitan Hotel, raised money among dining-room waiters.
Frederick Douglass contributed $200 and lectured for the relief society.
A large quantity of stores was received from England.
Mrs. Lincoln made frequent contributions to the relief society.
The President made contributions to the relief society.
In 1863, Elizabeth Keckley was re-elected President of the Contraband Relief Association.
For two years after Willie's death, the White House was the scene of no fashionable display.
Mrs. Lincoln was deeply affected by the memory of Willie's death.
The mention of Willie's name would excite Mrs. Lincoln's emotion.
Mrs. Lincoln could not bear to look upon Willie's picture.
After Willie's death, Mrs. Lincoln never crossed the threshold of the Guest's Room where he died.
Mrs. Lincoln never crossed the threshold of the Green Room where Willie was embalmed.
Tad's nature was the opposite of Willie's.
Tad was regarded as his father's favorite child.
Tad's black eyes sparkled with mischief.
In 1863, the Confederates were flushed with victory.
President Lincoln came into the room where Elizabeth Keckley was fitting a dress on Mrs. Lincoln.
Lincoln's step was slow and heavy, and his face was sad.
Lincoln threw himself upon a sofa and shaded his eyes with his hands.
Lincoln had come from the War Department.
Lincoln said there was 'plenty of news, but no good news. It is dark, dark everywhere.'
Lincoln took a small Bible from a stand near the head of the sofa.
Lincoln was reading the Book of Job.
After reading the Bible, Lincoln's face seemed more cheerful and lighted up with new resolution and hope.
Frequent letters were received warning Mr. Lincoln of assassination.
Lincoln never gave a second thought to warnings of assassination.
Mrs. Lincoln was sorely troubled by letters warning of assassination.
Mrs. Lincoln seemed to read impending danger in every rustling leaf and whisper of wind.
For weeks when trouble was anticipated, friends of the President would sleep in the White House to guard him.
Robert Lincoln would come home every few months.
Robert Lincoln was very anxious to quit school and enter the army.
Mrs. Lincoln sternly opposed Robert entering the army.
Mrs. Lincoln said 'We have lost one son, and his loss is as much as I can bear, without being called upon to make another sacrifice.'
Lincoln argued that Robert's services were required in the war.
Permission was eventually granted for Robert to enter the army.
Robert Lincoln entered the army with the rank of Captain and A.D.C.
Robert Lincoln remained in the army till the close of the war.
Tom Thumb's marriage to Miss Warren created excitement in Washington.
Some of Mrs. Lincoln's friends believed it was her duty to show attention to Tom Thumb.
Tom Thumb had been caressed by royalty in the Old World.
A hasty reception for Tom Thumb was arranged with cards of invitation issued.
Robert Lincoln refused to assist in entertaining Tom Thumb.
Robert Lincoln said his notions of duty were different from his mother's.
Robert Lincoln had a lofty soul and could not stoop to follies of fashionable life.
Mrs. Lincoln was extremely jealous of her husband.
Mrs. Lincoln's jealousy often was a source of perplexity to Mr. Lincoln.
Mrs. Lincoln would instruct Lincoln about which ladies he should and should not talk with at receptions.
Lincoln would respond to Mrs. Lincoln's jealous instructions with merry twinkle in his eyes.
Mr. and Mrs. Lincoln discussed the relations of Cabinet officers in Elizabeth Keckley's presence.
Mrs. Lincoln had no love for Secretary of the Treasury Salmon P. Chase.
Mrs. Lincoln was well versed in human character.
Mrs. Lincoln was somewhat suspicious of those by whom she was surrounded.
Mrs. Lincoln's judgment of people was often correct.
Mrs. Lincoln's intuition about the sincerity of individuals was more accurate than her husband's.
Mrs. Lincoln claimed that Chase was a selfish politician instead of a true patriot.
Mrs. Lincoln warned Mr. Lincoln not to trust Chase too far.
Miss Chase was quite a belle in Washington.
Mrs. Lincoln had no desire to build up Miss Chase's social position through political favor to her father.
Mr. Lincoln was more confiding than his wife.
Lincoln never suspected the fidelity of those who claimed to be his friends.
Lincoln was honest to the very core and frank as a child.
Lincoln never dreamed of questioning the sincerity of others.
Mrs. Lincoln warned Lincoln to inquire into the motives of Chase.
Lincoln said Chase was a patriot and one of his best friends.
Mrs. Lincoln said Chase would betray Lincoln if it were in his interest to do so.
Lincoln received letters daily from all parts of the country warning him not to trust Chase.
Lincoln attributed the warnings against Chase to political enemies of the Secretary.
Lincoln continued to confide in Chase until his tragic death.
Mrs. Lincoln was especially severe on Secretary of State William H. Seward.
Mrs. Lincoln rarely lost an opportunity to say an unkind word of Seward.
Elizabeth Keckley was a seamstress who worked for Mrs. Lincoln at the White House.
Mrs. Lincoln frequently criticized Secretary of State William H. Seward.
Mrs. Lincoln warned President Lincoln against trusting Secretary of the Treasury Salmon Chase.
President Lincoln continued to confide in Chase until his assassination.
Mrs. Lincoln opposed the appointment of Andrew Johnson as military Governor of Tennessee.
Mrs. Lincoln considered General McClellan a humbug despite his popularity with soldiers.
Mrs. Lincoln disliked General Grant and called him a butcher.
Mrs. Lincoln said she would leave the country if Grant were elected President.
Mrs. Lincoln's brothers served in the Confederate army.
Captain Alexander Todd, one of Mrs. Lincoln's brothers, was killed in the war.
Mrs. Lincoln had no sympathy for the South despite her family connections there.
President Lincoln respected the valor of Confederate generals including Lee, Jackson, and Johnston.
President Lincoln called Stonewall Jackson his ideal soldier.
On the morning of his assassination, President Lincoln praised General Lee's face as noble and brave.
President Lincoln urged his son Robert to leave the army and return to college to study law.
Elizabeth Keckley was president of a Relief Society.
Emancipated slaves came to Washington with exaggerated hopes about freedom.
Many freed slaves expressed regret about leaving the South and preferred their old associations.
Some freed slaves built cabins and cultivated gardens in the Freedmen's Village near Washington.
Elizabeth Keckley numbered twelve colored girls among her personal friends who worked as teachers in Washington schools.
In 1864, receptions resumed at the White House.
Mrs. Lincoln changed the custom of the President leading the promenade with a selected lady.
Mrs. Lincoln insisted on leading the promenade with the President to assert her position.
Mrs. Lincoln cultivated politicians around her to gain influence for Lincoln's re-election.
Mrs. Lincoln planned to drop her political associates after the election.
Mrs. Lincoln kept her political strategy hidden from President Lincoln.
President Lincoln was not handsome but was admired for his noble soul and great heart.
President Lincoln loved his wife tenderly and was sensitive to her moods.
Mrs. Lincoln incurred large debts buying expensive goods on credit.
Mrs. Lincoln was anxious about President Lincoln's re-election prospects.
President Lincoln was not indifferent to his wife.
Mrs. Lincoln often wounded President Lincoln in unguarded moments.
Mrs. Lincoln was extremely anxious that her husband be re-elected President.
Mrs. Lincoln incurred many expenses in attempting to make a display becoming her exalted position.
Mr. Lincoln's salary was inadequate to meet Mrs. Lincoln's expenses.
Mrs. Lincoln was forced to run into debt.
Mrs. Lincoln bought expensive goods on credit.
By summer 1864, Mrs. Lincoln faced enormous unpaid bills.
Elizabeth Keckley believed Mr. Lincoln would be re-elected.
Mrs. Lincoln had contracted large debts of which Mr. Lincoln knew nothing.
Mrs. Lincoln owed approximately twenty-seven thousand dollars.
The principal portion of Mrs. Lincoln's debts was at Stewart's in New York.
Mrs. Lincoln believed Mr. Lincoln had little idea of the expense of a woman's wardrobe.
Mrs. Lincoln felt she must dress in costly materials because people scrutinized every article she wore.
Mrs. Lincoln's upbringing in the West subjected her to more searching observation.
Mr. Lincoln is too honest to make a penny outside of his salary.
Mrs. Lincoln feared Mr. Lincoln would go mad if he knew the extent of her debts.
Mrs. Lincoln feared politicians would use her debts against her husband in the Presidential campaign.
Elizabeth Keckley did not approve of ladies attached to the Presidential household coming to her rooms.
Elizabeth Keckley believed it would be more consistent with their dignity for them to send for her.
Elizabeth Keckley asked Mrs. Lincoln for the right-hand glove President Lincoln wore at the first public reception after his second inaugural.
Elizabeth Keckley valued President Lincoln as a liberator of her race.
The election came off and the loyal States decided Mr. Lincoln should continue as President.
The second inauguration of Abraham Lincoln took place on a dark and gloomy morning.
When President Lincoln stepped forward to receive the oath of office, clouds parted and sunshine streamed upon his face.
A brilliant star was reportedly seen at noon-day during the inauguration.
The second inauguration was on Saturday.
President Lincoln told Elizabeth Keckley the position brings with it many trials.
President Lincoln said he put his trust in God.
Mrs. Lincoln expressed concern that President Lincoln was looking broken-hearted and worn out.
Mrs. Lincoln feared President Lincoln would not get through the next four years.
The first grand levee after the inauguration was one of the largest receptions ever held in Washington.
Orders were issued not to admit people of color to the levee.
Frederick Douglass was initially prevented from entering the White House levee due to his race.
A member of Congress helped Frederick Douglass gain entry to meet President Lincoln.
President Lincoln told Frederick Douglass he had long admired his course and valued his opinions highly.
Frederick Douglass was very proud of the manner in which President Lincoln received him.
Mrs. Lincoln was not introduced to Frederick Douglass at the levee.
Mrs. Lincoln entered the inaugural ball leaning on the arm of Senator Sumner.
The inaugural ball was the last time President Lincoln and his wife appeared in public together.
Richmond fell on April 3rd.
Elizabeth Keckley had promised her employees a holiday when Richmond should fall.
It was reported that Richmond had surrendered to colored troops.
Mrs. Lincoln invited Elizabeth Keckley to accompany her to City Point.
City Point was near Petersburg, Elizabeth Keckley's old home.
Mrs. Lincoln was joined by Mrs. Secretary Harlan and daughter, Senator Sumner, and several other gentlemen on the steamer to City Point.
President Lincoln had visited Richmond, Petersburg, and other points before the party arrived at City Point.
Mrs. Lincoln was disappointed that President Lincoln had visited Richmond without her.
The entire party visited Richmond and other points with the President.
The River Queen steamed up James River, which had long been impassable even to Union gunboats.
Elizabeth Keckley stood on deck viewing the landscape and reflecting on changes since she had last seen Virginia.
Elizabeth Keckley wondered about the fate of people she once knew in Virginia.
In Richmond's Capitol, Elizabeth Keckley found papers including a resolution prohibiting all free colored people from entering Virginia.
Elizabeth Keckley was born in Virginia.
Elizabeth Keckley valued her birthplace despite the circumstances of her birth.
Elizabeth Keckley accompanied the Presidential party to Richmond after the fall of the Confederacy.
Elizabeth Keckley picked up papers in the Capitol building in Richmond, including a resolution prohibiting free colored people from entering Virginia.
Elizabeth Keckley sat in Jefferson Davis's chair in the Senate chamber.
Elizabeth Keckley sat in Vice-President Alexander H. Stephens's chair.
Elizabeth Keckley was present at a dinner on the River Queen where Mrs. Lincoln became offended by a young officer's remarks.
Elizabeth Keckley accompanied the Presidential party to Petersburg.
Elizabeth Keckley had known people in Petersburg from her youth.
Elizabeth Keckley found that war had brought many changes to Petersburg.
Elizabeth Keckley was present when President Lincoln observed a terrapin on the way back from Petersburg.
Elizabeth Keckley spent a week on the River Queen at City Point and found it delightful.
Elizabeth Keckley had never heard Mr. Lincoln make a public speech before the evening of his address at the White House.
Elizabeth Keckley asked Mrs. Lincoln for permission to attend Mr. Lincoln's speech at the White House.
Elizabeth Keckley asked permission to bring a friend to hear Mr. Lincoln speak.
Elizabeth Keckley dressed Mrs. Lincoln before the President's speech.
Elizabeth Keckley observed that it would be easy to kill the President as he stood at the window giving his speech.
Elizabeth Keckley mentioned her concerns about the President's safety to Mrs. Lincoln the day after the speech.
Mrs. Lincoln told Elizabeth Keckley that she had a presentiment Mr. Lincoln would meet with a sudden and violent end.
Mr. Lincoln was fond of pets, particularly two goats.
Mr. Lincoln and Tad played with the goats in the yard on warm bright days.
Mr. Lincoln asked Elizabeth Keckley if she was fond of pets.
Mr. Lincoln expressed contempt for bounty-jumpers and those who manipulated the system.
Mrs. Lincoln was not fond of pets.
Mrs. Lincoln could not bear the sight of things that Willie loved after his death.
Mrs. Lincoln threw away costly bouquets and Willie's toys because they reminded her of her dead son.
Elizabeth Keckley described Mrs. Lincoln as a peculiarly constituted woman.
Mr. Lincoln's goats were given away after his death, believed to have gone to Mrs. Lee.
Elizabeth Keckley made her home with Mr. and Mrs. Walker Lewis during her residence in the Capital.
Elizabeth Keckley saw Mrs. Lincoln only briefly on Friday morning, April 14th.
Mrs. Lincoln told Elizabeth Keckley she would attend the theatre that night with the President.
Elizabeth Keckley was not summoned to assist Mrs. Lincoln in dressing for the theatre on April 14th.
Elizabeth Keckley was not summoned to assist Mrs. Lincoln with her toilette on the night of Lincoln's assassination.
Elizabeth Keckley felt that the blood had frozen in her veins when she heard that President Lincoln had been shot.
Elizabeth Keckley could not remain in a state of uncertainty after learning of the President's shooting.
Elizabeth Keckley felt that the house would not hold her in her distress.
Elizabeth Keckley walked rapidly towards the White House after learning of the assassination.
Elizabeth Keckley could not sleep the night of Lincoln's assassination.
Elizabeth Keckley wanted to go to Mrs. Lincoln but did not know where to find her.
Elizabeth Keckley held her arms in mental agony throughout the night.
Elizabeth Keckley was summoned to the White House by Mrs. Lincoln on Saturday morning.
Elizabeth Keckley hastily put on her shawl and bonnet when summoned to the White House.
Elizabeth Keckley was driven at a rapid rate to the White House.
Mrs. Lincoln had sent for Elizabeth Keckley the night of the assassination.
Three messengers were successively dispatched to find Elizabeth Keckley but all mistook the number of her residence.
Elizabeth Keckley found Mrs. Lincoln tossing uneasily on a bed when she arrived at the White House.
Mrs. Secretary Welles had spent the night with Mrs. Lincoln.
Elizabeth Keckley laid her hand upon Mrs. Lincoln's hot brow.
Mrs. Welles excused herself to go to her own family, leaving Elizabeth Keckley alone with Mrs. Lincoln.
Elizabeth Keckley received permission to go into the Guests' Room where the President's body lay in state.
Elizabeth Keckley recalled seeing little Willie Lincoln in his coffin in the same room.
Elizabeth Keckley remembered the President weeping over his son Willie's pale face.
Elizabeth Keckley entered the solemn chamber of death with a palpitating heart and trembling footsteps.
Elizabeth Keckley referred to Lincoln as the Moses of her people.
Elizabeth Keckley had worshipped Lincoln as an idol and looked upon him as a demi-god.
Elizabeth Keckley lifted the white cloth from Lincoln's white face.
Members of the Cabinet and distinguished army officers were grouped around Lincoln's body.
The Cabinet members and officers made room for Elizabeth Keckley to approach the body.
